perturbation.original_text: g. Governing Law and Forum. This Agreement will for all purposes be governed by and interpreted in accordance with the laws of the State of California without giving effect to any conflict of laws principles that require the application of the laws of a different state. Each of the parties hereto (i) irrevocably agrees that the federal and state courts in the Northern District of California shall have sole and exclusive jurisdiction over any suit or other proceeding arising out of or based upon this Agreement, (ii) submits to the venue and jurisdiction of such courts, and (iii) irrevocably consents to personal jurisdiction by such courts.
perturbation.changed_text: g. Governing Law and Forum. This Agreement will for all purposes be governed by and interpreted in accordance with the laws of the State of Delaware without giving effect to any conflict of laws principles that require the application of the laws of a different state. Each of the parties hereto (i) agrees that the federal and state courts in the Southern District of New York shall have jurisdiction over any suit or other proceeding arising out of or based upon this Agreement, (ii) submits to the venue and jurisdiction of such courts, and (iii) consents to personal jurisdiction by such courts.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law and forum clause creates uncertainty and potential legal challenges. The original clause clearly specifies California law and the Northern District of California courts. By changing it to Delaware law and the Southern District of New York courts, parties might be confused about where and under what laws disputes will be resolved, thus increasing complexity.
perturbation.law_explanation: This change contradicts 28 U.S.C. § 1391, which dictates the proper venue for civil actions. By arbitrarily changing the forum selection to the Southern District of New York, the contract might violate the statute if the parties, property, or events related to the contract lack sufficient connection to that district, potentially leading to challenges regarding venue.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 28 U.S. Code § 1391 - Venue generally | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 28 U.S. Code § 1391 - Venue generally U.S. Code Notes prev | next (a) Applicability of Section.— Except as otherwise provided by law— (1) this section shall govern the venue of all civil actions brought in district courts of the United States; and (2) the proper venue for a civil action shall be determined without regard to whether the action is local or transitory in nature. (b) Venue in General.— A civil action may be brought in— (1) a judicial district in which any defendant resides, if all defendants are residents of the State in which the district is located; (2) a judicial district in which a substantial part of the events or omissions giving rise to the claim occurred, or a substantial part of property that is the subject of the action is situated; or (3) if there is no district in which an action may otherwise be brought as provided in this section, any judicial district in which any defendant is subject to the court’s personal jurisdiction with respect to such action. (c) Residency.— For all venue purposes— (1) a natural person, including an alien lawfully admitted for permanent residence in the United States, shall be deemed to reside in the judicial district in which that person is domiciled; (2) an entity with the capacity to sue and be sued in its common name under applicable law, whether or not incorporated, shall be deemed to reside, if a defendant, in any judicial district in which such defendant is subject to the court’s personal jurisdiction with respect to the civil action in question and, if a plaintiff, only in the judicial district in which it maintains its principal place of business; and (3) a defendant not resident in the United States may be sued in any judicial district, and the joinder of such a defendant shall be disregarded in determining where the action may be brought w
perturbation.justification: The change in the contract directly contradicts 28 U.S.C. § 1391 by altering the venue for civil actions. The original text specifies California law and the Northern District of California, while the changed text designates Delaware law and the Southern District of New York. According to the Law Explanation, this could violate 28 U.S.C. § 1391 if the parties, property, or events related to the contract lack sufficient connection to the Southern District of New York. Scraped Law Snippet 2 states that "(b) Venue in General.— A civil action may be brought in— (1) a judicial district in which any defendant resides, if all defendants are residents of the State in which the district is located; (2) a judicial district in which a substantial part of the events or omissions giving rise to the claim occurred, or a substantial part of property that is the subject of the action is situated." The change introduces a structural flaw by potentially violating the venue requirements outlined in the statute, creating a clear contradiction because the chosen venue may not have proper jurisdiction.

perturbation.original_text: i. Severability. In case any one or more of the terms contained in this Agreement shall be invalid, illegal or unenforceable in any respect, the validity, legality and enforceability of the remaining terms shall not in any way be affected or impaired thereby. The parties shall endeavor in good faith negotiations to replace the invalid, illegal or unenforceable terms with valid terms the economic effect of which comes as close as possible to that of the invalid, illegal or unenforceable terms.
perturbation.changed_text: i. Severability. In case any one or more of the terms contained in this Agreement shall be invalid, illegal or unenforceable in any respect, the validity, legality and enforceability of the remaining terms shall be affected and impaired thereby, rendering the entire agreement void.
perturbation.explanation: Altering the severability clause such that if any term is unenforceable, the entire agreement is void introduces legal risk. Standard severability clauses protect the contract by ensuring that valid terms remain in effect even if others are not. Making the entire agreement contingent on each term's enforceability makes the contract susceptible to challenges and potential invalidation, particularly if minor or technical clauses are deemed unenforceable.
perturbation.law_explanation: By stating that the entire agreement is void if any part is unenforceable, the modified text contradicts the principle of severability as outlined in Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 184. This section promotes upholding the valid portions of a contract even if some parts are invalid, whereas the modification makes the entire contract contingent on the enforceability of each individual term, which is contrary to established contract law principles.

perturbation.justification: The modification to the severability clause directly contradicts the principle of severability, which aims to uphold the valid portions of a contract even if some parts are unenforceable. The changed text makes the entire contract void if any term is unenforceable, reversing the function of a standard severability clause. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 is missing, Scraped Law Snippet 2 appears to be a login page for a legal repository and doesn't directly offer legal content pertinent to this issue. However, the Law Explanation, combined with the Perturbation Explanation and a comparison of the original and changed texts, clarifies that the altered clause undermines established contract law principles designed to preserve agreements as much as possible.

perturbation.original_text: EXCEPT IN CASES OF GROSS NEGLIGENCE, WILLFUL MISCONDUCT OR FRAUD, INDEMNIFICATION CLAIMS UNDER SECTION 5 OR BREACHES OF SECTION 2 (TRADEMARKS), 8 (CONFIDENTIALITY), OR 9 (NO AGENCY RELATIONSHIP), IN NO EVENT SHALL EITHER PARTY OR ITS OFFICERS, DIRECTORS, OR EMPLOYEES BE LIABLE TO THE OTHER PARTY IN CONNECTION WITH THE SUBJECT MATTER HEREOF, FOR ANY SPECIAL, INDIRECT, CONSEQUENTIAL OR PUNITIVE DAMAGES OF ANY KIND, LOST PROFITS OR LOST REVENUE, WHETHER ARISING IN CONTRACT, TORT, NEGLIGENCE, STATUTE, OR OTHERWISE, EVEN IF ADVISED OF THE POSSIBILITY THEREOF. IN NO EVENT SHALL THE NON-BREACHING PARTY BE ENTITLED TO EQUITABLE OR INJUNCTIVE RELIEF OF ANY KIND.
perturbation.changed_text: IN NO EVENT SHALL EITHER PARTY OR ITS OFFICERS, DIRECTORS, OR EMPLOYEES BE LIABLE TO THE OTHER PARTY IN CONNECTION WITH THE SUBJECT MATTER HEREOF, FOR ANY SPECIAL, INDIRECT, CONSEQUENTIAL OR PUNITIVE DAMAGES OF ANY KIND, LOST PROFITS OR LOST REVENUE, WHETHER ARISING IN CONTRACT, TORT, NEGLIGENCE, STATUTE, OR OTHERWISE, EVEN IF ADVISED OF THE POSSIBILITY THEREOF. IN NO EVENT SHALL THE NON-BREACHING PARTY BE ENTITLED TO EQUITABLE OR INJUNCTIVE RELIEF OF ANY KIND.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the exceptions for gross negligence, willful misconduct, fraud, indemnification claims, and breaches of certain sections significantly limits liability. This can create a situation where a party engaging in egregious behavior would not be held liable for damages, contradicting general legal principles regarding accountability and potentially violating public policy.
perturbation.law_explanation: Removing the carve-outs for gross negligence, willful misconduct, and fraud arguably violates California Civil Code § 1668, which states that contracts that exempt one from responsibility for their own fraud, willful injury to person or property, or violation of law are against public policy. By removing these exceptions, the clause could be interpreted as attempting to shield a party from liability even for intentional or grossly negligent actions, which is prohibited under California law.

OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 2. CONTRACTS [1549 - 1701] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 4. UNLAWFUL CONTRACTS [1667 - 1670.12] ( Title 4 enacted 1872. ) 1668. All contracts which have for their object, directly or indirectly, to exempt any one from responsibility for his own fraud, or willful injury to the person or property of another, or violation of law, whether willful or negligent, are against the policy of the law. (Enacted 1872.)
perturbation.justification: The modified contract removes exceptions for gross negligence, willful misconduct, and fraud. This contradicts California Civil Code § 1668, as stated in the Law Explanation, because it attempts to exempt a party from responsibility for their own fraud or willful injury. Scraped Law Snippet 1 states, "All contracts which have for their object, directly or indirectly, to exempt any one from responsibility for his own fraud, or willful injury to the person or property of another, or violation of law...are against the policy of the law." The changed text, by removing the exceptions, directly violates this code, making the contradiction explicit and impactful. The modified clause would likely fail legal scrutiny because it seeks to shield a party from liability for intentional or grossly negligent actions, which is against public policy as defined in the scraped snippet.
